questions.text: Does the response focus on the person who made the claim (their character, motive, or past), or does it engage the claim itself?

Does the conclusion rest mainly on something other than reasons (a feeling, a name, a head-count, a label like 'natural', or 'I can't see how it could be so'), or is there evidence and reasoning you could examine?

Does the support point somewhere other than the conclusion (restating the claim, citing timing alone, making a sweeping leap, or resting on what the speaker can't picture), or does it actually bear on it?
Is the issue framed as just two paths when a middle ground or other options genuinely exist, or are the available choices presented fairly?
After the response, is the original claim left standing while attention moves to the speaker, or has the claim actually been addressed?
Is a reason to distrust the speaker being offered as if it settles whether their claim is true, or is the claim weighed on its own?
Is the version being argued against a more extreme or absolute version of the claim, or the one actually made?
Would the person who made the claim say the restatement of their position has been changed on a key point, or would they recognize and accept it?
Are the two options presented genuinely the only ones, or is a compromise, a 'both', or a 'neither' realistically available?
Is a workable middle ground or 'do both' option being treated as if it weren't on the table, or are all the realistic positions actually represented?
Are the steps from the first move to the feared outcome treated as certain to follow without support, or shown to be likely?
Is the feared end result much larger and more alarming than the small step being discussed, or close to it in scale?
Is the claim supported mainly by who endorsed it (a respected person, expert, or celebrity), or by reasons you could check?
Is the cited source speaking outside their field while being treated as the whole case, or does their expertise actually fit this topic?
Is a sweeping 'always/everyone' claim resting on one case or a small handful, or does the range of examples match how broad the conclusion is?
Are the examples cherry-picked or anecdotal cases standing in for the whole group, or do they look like a representative range?
Does the support already assume the conclusion is true (the reason and the claim restating each other), or is it an independent reason?
Does accepting the premise require already accepting the conclusion, or would the argument persuade someone who doesn't already agree?
If you set aside the emotional pull (fear, pity, anger), was the feeling carrying the conclusion, or is there still a reason left that supports it?
Is the conclusion pressed mainly by stirring a strong feeling, or by reasons that would still stand if the case were put calmly?
Does the case rest only on the two things happening together or one before the other, or is there a mechanism or evidence for the cause?
Is a correlation read directly as cause, or are other explanations (coincidence, a shared third cause, reverse direction) considered and ruled out?
Is the critic's own conduct or inconsistency being used to set the criticism aside, or is the criticism itself being answered on its merits?
Is the pointed-out inconsistency raised only to deflect the point, or is it genuinely relevant because consistency or a double standard is the actual question?
Is 'natural' (or 'artificial/man-made') being used as the main reason something is good or bad, or is there separate evidence of its actual effects?
Does the argument treat naturalness alone as settling it across the board, or does it check the specific case for benefit or harm?
Is 'natural means good' (or 'man-made means harmful') doing the persuading here, or is the verdict tied to measured benefit or harm in this case?
Is how many people believe or do it offered as the reason it's true or right, or as one clue alongside independent reasons?
Is going along with the crowd treated as the reason to agree, or is independent evidence given beyond 'most people' or 'everyone' agreeing?
Is the choice boxed into exactly two camps when other positions are open, or are the realistic options all on the table?
Is each step from the first move toward the extreme treated as bound to follow, or is the chain shown to be likely at each link?
Does the case lean on the prestige of the name attached to it, or on reasons that would hold without that name?
Does the reason given simply restate the conclusion in other words, or add something the conclusion did not already say?
Is the push toward the conclusion mainly an emotional pull, or a reason that holds once the feeling is set aside?
Is 'one came first, so it caused the other' the whole case, or is a mechanism or ruling-out of other causes also given?
Is being natural (or not man-made) treated as settling that it is good or safe, or is that judged by its measured effects?
Is the claim rejected because the speaker can't picture or understand how it could be true, or because of evidence that it isn't?
Is 'I don't see how it could be so' being treated as showing it's false, or is an actual reason against the claim given?
Does the doubt point to a gap in the speaker's own knowledge or imagination, or to something genuinely implausible about the claim itself?
Is the claim dismissed without looking into whether anyone could explain it, or only after a real attempt to check was made?
Is the source characterized unfavorably before they make their point, or is their point taken up after it is actually made?
Is the audience told to set aside whatever this person will say in advance, or is each thing they say left open to be judged on its own?
Is the claim's worth settled by where or whom it originally came from, or by what the claim itself actually says now?
Is the idea's origin or history treated as settling whether it's true today, or is its present merit examined regardless of how it started?
Does the reply move to a different topic than the one under discussion, or stay on the original point?
Would settling the introduced point leave the original question unanswered, or does that point bear on the original question?
Does the reply raise a separate grievance about another party, or respond to the criticism on its own terms?
After the counter-grievance, does the original charge remain unaddressed, or has it been engaged?
Once the requested evidence was supplied, was a new and higher requirement introduced, or was the original standard accepted as met?
Was the added demand absent from the criteria stated up front, or part of the standard all along?
Is there a gap where the conclusion does not follow from the reasons offered, or do the reasons support it?
Is the step connecting reasons to conclusion absent, or spelled out or clearly implied?
Is the conclusion urged mainly by stressing how frightening an outcome would be, or by evidence that the outcome is actually likely?
Is the danger painted as bigger or more certain than what's shown, or is its scale and likelihood backed by what's known?
Is acceptance urged mainly by stirring sympathy for someone's hardship, or by reasons the conclusion holds on its own?
Is the suffering brought up as the reason to agree when it doesn't bear on the question, or is it genuinely relevant to what's being decided?
Is the position dismissed by making it sound absurd or laughable, or by a reason showing it's actually wrong?
Is the position presented in a mocking caricature to invite a laugh, or engaged seriously as it was meant?
Is agreement urged by the satisfaction of seeing a disliked person or group lose or suffer, or by reasons the conclusion is right?
Is the target's bad conduct invoked to stir ill-will when it doesn't bear on the question, or is that conduct genuinely the matter being decided?
Is the case resting mainly on how long-established or traditional the practice is, or on reasons it actually works?
Does it point to what the long practice has produced, or to the fact that it has lasted a long time?
Is the case resting mainly on how new, modern, or up-to-date the thing is, or on reasons it actually performs better?
Does it point to a real improvement over the older option, or to the fact that this one is the latest?
Is the recommendation resting mainly on the elite or discerning status of who chooses it, or on the thing's actual qualities?
Is the pull mainly the promise of joining a select set, or reasons the thing is genuinely good?
Are the two things treated as equivalent while a large difference in their scale or degree is passed over, or is the comparison made between things that are genuinely similar in size and seriousness?
Is one trait the two things share being used to call them the same overall, or is the comparison limited to the specific respect in which they actually match?
Is the option set aside because it doesn't solve the whole problem or has some flaw, or because its remaining flaws genuinely outweigh the improvement it offers?
Is a flawless or complete fix being treated as the only acceptable standard, or is the option judged against the realistic alternatives actually on offer?
Is the middle position taken as right mainly because it falls between the two sides, or is it backed by its own reasons regardless of where it sits?
Is the answer fixed at a split-the-difference point because both sides made a claim, or is the better-supported side allowed to be right even if it's one of the extremes?
Is a premise the conclusion depends on taken for granted, or is that premise itself given support?
Is the very point in dispute built into the argument as an already-settled assumption, or is it argued for openly?
Does the question treat an unproven point as already true, or does it leave that point genuinely open?
Would any direct answer to the question concede the contested assumption, or can it be answered without granting it?
Is the lack of evidence against the claim being treated as a reason it's true, or is there positive evidence offered for it?
Is 'it hasn't been proven' being taken to show the claim is false, or is its truth left open until evidence comes in?
Is the person who made the claim asking others to disprove it, or are they offering support for it themselves?
Is the claim being treated as standing by default until someone refutes it, or does it earn acceptance by being backed up?
Is the conclusion drawn from a particular source not mentioning or recording the thing, or from what that source actually states?
Is the missing mention treated as telling without showing that source would mention it, or is it established the source would surely have noted it?
Is a personal story or single experience offered as the support, or is there systematic evidence about the wider pattern?
Does one striking case override the broader statistics, or do the wider numbers carry the conclusion?
Is known evidence pointing the other way left out, or is the contrary evidence acknowledged and weighed?
Are only the cases that fit the conclusion put forward, or is the full mix of supporting and conflicting cases shown?
Was the group looked at gathered in a skewed way (volunteers, a handy crowd, one slice), or drawn to fairly represent the whole?
Do the people or cases sampled differ from the population the conclusion is about, or do they match it?
Is the rule stated as holding always or never with no exceptions, or is it qualified to where it actually holds?
Is the broad rule applied to a case that's a known exception, or does it allow for cases that don't fit?
Is the order in time (it happened after, so it was caused by) the main support offered, or is there evidence beyond the sequence?
Did the before-then-after happen just once as a single episode, or has the same sequence been seen to recur reliably?
Is a single cause named as the whole explanation, or is the outcome treated as having several contributing factors?
Are other plausible contributing causes left out as if they didn't matter, or acknowledged and weighed?
Does the case rest on two things rising and falling together as a pattern, or on evidence that one actually produces the other?
Is the direction (which one causes which) simply assumed, or established so it couldn't equally run the other way?
Does the comparison lean on one or two surface resemblances, or on shared traits that genuinely bear on the conclusion?
Does the conclusion need deeper similarity to hold, or could it stand on the single trait being pointed at alone?
Is there a difference between the two cases that bears directly on the conclusion, or are they alike in the respect that matters most?
Does the parallel break down at the exact point the argument turns on, or does it hold right there?
Are the two things measured against one shared standard that fits both, or lined up across different units, scales, or categories?
Are the two too different in kind for a side-by-side to mean anything, or are they the same kind of thing for the purpose at hand?
Does a key word carry one meaning in the premise and a different meaning in the conclusion, or does it hold the same sense throughout?
If the key term were fixed to a single definition for the whole argument, would the conclusion stop following, or would it still hold?
Does the argument lean on a sentence that grammar or sentence structure lets you read in two different ways, or is the phrasing read only one way?
Is the load-bearing sentence left structured so its meaning stays uncertain, or is it rephrased so only one reading survives?
Does the point depend on stressing particular words or pulling a quote out of context, or does it hold under the statement's plain emphasis?
Would removing the added emphasis or restoring the surrounding context change what the statement means, or would it read the same?
Is the key term defined in a way that makes the conclusion follow automatically, or is it defined independently of what's being argued?
Does the offered definition depart from the term's ordinary or agreed meaning to favor one side, or does it match how the word is normally used?
Is a trait held by each part being carried up to the whole they form, or is the whole's trait shown on its own?
Is the part-to-whole transfer just assumed, or is there a reason that property survives combining the parts?
Is a trait held by the whole being pushed down onto each part, or is the part's trait shown on its own?
Is the whole-to-part transfer just assumed, or is there a reason that property reaches each individual member?
Is a group average or rate being read as a fact about a specific individual, or is the claim kept at the group level?
Is the conclusion about the person resting only on the group statistic, or is there evidence about that individual?
Does it move from 'the predicted result is here' straight to 'the stated condition is true', or does it allow the same result could arrive without that condition?
Are other ways the same result could have come about left unconsidered, or are they checked and set aside before settling on the stated condition?
Does it conclude the result cannot happen just because the stated condition is absent, or does it allow the result might still arrive by another route?
Is the stated condition treated as the single path to the result, or is it shown to be the only way the result could occur?
Does the conclusion rest on the test result or sign by itself, or does it also weigh how common the thing being concluded actually is?
When a rare thing gets a positive result, is a positive simply taken to mean it's almost surely true, or is the rarity folded into the odds?
For events that don't influence each other, is a recent streak treated as changing the next outcome's odds, or are the odds taken as the same each time?
After a run of one result, is the opposite called 'due' to come up, or is each independent trial left at its own fixed chance?
Was the case picked because it was at an unusual high or low point, or chosen in a way unrelated to how extreme it was?
Is an extreme case's drift back toward its normal level credited to an action taken, or is ordinary settling-back ruled out as the explanation?
Does the evidence come only from the cases that succeeded or remained, or are the ones that failed or dropped out also counted?
Could the missing failures share the very trait being praised, or has it been checked that the lost cases wouldn't overturn the lesson?
Was the pattern or boundary chosen after the data was seen, or stated in advance before looking?
Is one striking cluster singled out while the spots where nothing lined up are set aside, or are all the cases weighed together?
Does the case rest on the outcome of believing it being good, or on evidence that the claim itself is true?
Is the claim set aside because its implication would be unwelcome, or because something shows it to be wrong?
Is the claim set aside because of what the speaker stands to gain, or because of what the evidence shows?
Does pointing at the speaker's stake stand in for checking the point, or does the point still get examined on its own?
Is the act defended by pointing to a similar wrong elsewhere, or by reasons the act itself is acceptable?
Is 'it has been done before' treated as cancelling the fault, or is the act still judged on its own?
Is the claim set aside because the speaker's situation makes it the view they'd hold, or is the view itself addressed?
Is 'of course they'd say that, given who they are' used to explain the view away, or to flag a bias still worth checking?
Are two or more separate matters bundled into one question, or is a single matter being asked about?
Would one answer be forced to cover both matters at once, or can each part be answered on its own?
Is a counterexample dismissed by calling it 'not a true member' of the group, or is the example allowed to count against the claim?
Was the membership requirement added only after the counterexample came up, or was it set out beforehand?
Is one option redefined so broadly that its opposite has almost nothing left to cover, or do both sides still apply to real cases?
Does the widened meaning make the contrast useless because almost everything now counts as one side, or does the distinction still draw a real line?
Is the main thing pushing you to accept the claim a threatened cost (penalty, harm, lost standing), or a reason the claim is true?
Is disagreeing framed as bringing trouble onto you, or is disagreeing left free to be judged on the merits?
Does the case rely on complimenting the listener to bring them around, or on reasons that would persuade a stranger getting no praise?
If the flattery were stripped out, would the claim be left unsupported, or would real reasons still stand behind it?
Is the high price or cost being used as the proof of quality, or is quality shown by how the thing actually performs?
Is someone's riches treated as evidence they're correct, or is their claim weighed on its own reasons regardless of their wealth?
Is 'it's just common sense' or 'it's obvious' standing in for support, or are actual reasons offered for the claim?
Is doubting the claim treated as foolish or lacking sense, or is the doubt met with an actual answer?
Do the word choices themselves deliver the judgment, or is the judgment backed by stated reasons apart from the wording?
If the chosen terms were swapped for plain neutral ones, would the point lose its force, or would it stand on the same facts?
Is the backing an unnamed, general attribution (such as 'some say' or 'studies show'), or is a specific source named that one could check?
Does a soft qualifier ('arguably', 'many believe', 'it is widely held') stand in for support, or is there actual evidence behind it?
Is the lack of an exact dividing line treated as proof the two ends are the same, or is the real gap between the clear cases still granted?
Does the argument chain tiny indistinguishable steps to collapse a genuine distinction, or does it accept that small steps can still add up to a real difference?
Is an abstraction (like 'society', 'the market', or 'nature') spoken of as a thing that wants, decides, or acts, or is it used as plain shorthand for the people or forces behind it?
Does the conclusion rest on the abstraction being a literal entity, or does it rest on the concrete people, events, or numbers it stands for?
